Title: Themes and global themes
Summary: Eight built-in color themes, plus a fork-a-built-in editor for custom themes. Bundle a color theme with a font into a named global theme.
Body:
## Eight built-in color themes

[Preferences → Color theme](/preferences) ships with eight built-in themes: Dark, Light, Red, Amber, Green, Cyan, Blue, Magenta. Each one re-points the accent palette and re-tints the neutral grays toward the same hue. The active theme is captured in a `data-theme` attribute on the document root and resolves through CSS variables, so the change is instant and applies everywhere (including [scrollbars](/help/editor-basics/scrollbars)).

## Custom color themes

You can fork any built-in theme into a custom one. The Color theme picker has three buttons next to the dropdown:

- **+ New** asks whether to fork from the current theme or start blank (dark), then prompts for a name.
- **Edit** opens an inline 7-row editor for the active custom theme. Five ladder pickers (accent, neutral, source-NPC, source-Terminal, choice — each picked color becomes the 500 stop, and the other 10 stops derive at render time via CSS `color-mix`) and two single-color pickers (canvas background, canvas pattern). Each row is pre-populated from the theme's currently effective color so you can see what you're forking before you change it.
- **Delete** removes the active custom theme and rewires anything referencing it back to its base built-in.

Clicking **Edit** on a built-in theme auto-forks it into a fresh custom theme on first click, so you can edit a built-in without touching a separate "new" flow.

## Global themes

A global theme is a named bundle of color theme (built-in OR custom) + body font + UI scale + letter spacing. The default global theme is built in and cannot be deleted; you can save your current settings as a new one through Preferences and switch between them.

This is useful when you want to reach for "the look I had yesterday" without remembering each individual setting. One custom color theme can also be referenced by multiple global themes (e.g. same colors, different fonts).

## Cross-window sync

Theme changes propagate across every open Bleepforge window via a same-origin BroadcastChannel. Change the theme in the Preferences popout and the main window updates in the same paint.

## Where it is stored

Global themes and custom color themes live in `data/preferences.json`. They are also cached to `localStorage` so the app paints with the right theme before the server response arrives. Without that cache, you would see a flash of dark theme on every page load before the saved theme applied.
